data.title: Manulife becomes first insurer to redomicile in Hong Kong
data.description:
Manulife (International), one of the biggest life insurers and pension providers in Hong Kong and Macau, has finished moving its domicile to Hong Kong from Bermuda, making it the first insurer to successfully complete the process under a new regime, according to a statement on Friday.
The insurer said the redomiciling was a “strategic milestone” that underscored its confidence in Hong Kong’s role as a leading international financial centre and reinforced the company’s long-standing commitment to the city’s insurance market.
“This move reflects our deep-rooted belief in Hong Kong’s future and its importance to our business,” said Patrick Graham, CEO of Manulife Hong Kong and Macau. “Re-domiciling locally strengthens our alignment with Hong Kong’s robust regulatory framework and enables us to better serve our 2.6 million customers in both Hong Kong and Macau, advancing our ambition to be the No 1 choice for customers.”
Manulife is the first insurer to redomicile in Hong Kong after a law change in May that made the process simpler and easier. The legislation allows companies that are incorporated overseas to establish themselves in the city while maintaining their legal identity and business continuity.
Previously, a company that wanted to move its domicile had to close an existing entity and shift all assets and transactions to a new firm in Hong Kong.
French insurer AXA’s local unit began the process on the law’s first day of implementation, but Manulife is the first insurer to complete it.
Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury Christopher Hui Ching-yu said on social media on Friday that he had met Manulife executives during a trip to Canada in May to promote the new legislation.
Market response to the new regime had been strong, Hui said, as the Companies Registry had approved four cases and was processing 20 applications from financial firms, holding companies, insurance companies and fund houses.
“This reflects that these companies have confidence in the business environment of Hong Kong,” Hui said.
The Insurance Authority welcomed Manulife’s re-domiciliation, which reflected “the strengths and allure of our market, driven by Hong Kong’s position as an international financial centre, and connectivity among cities in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macau Greater Bay Area”, the regulator said in a statement on Friday.
“The Insurance Authority will continue to work closely with all relevant parties to enable interested insurers to redomicile to Hong Kong under the streamlined and cost-effective regime, supporting the government’s vision to strengthen the headquarters economy.”
In October, a unit of PetroChina Investment (Hong Kong) became the first company approved to reincorporate in the city under the new law.
In September, two Hong Kong-listed companies under China Resources Group announced plans to redomicile from the Cayman Islands.
The pair are China Resources Beverage (Holdings), the country’s second-largest water bottler under the brand C’estbon, and its sister company, China Resources Building Materials Technology Holdings.

data.title: Hongkong Land poised to launch US$6 billion Singapore property fund
data.description:
Hongkong Land is set to launch what it expects to be the largest private real estate fund in Singapore, with more than S$8 billion (US$6.2 billion) in assets under management, including prime commercial projects in the waterfront Marina Bay district.
At inception, the Singapore Central Private Real Estate Fund (SCPREF) would include one-third stakes in One Raffles Quay and Marina Bay Financial Centre Towers 1 and 2, the Hong Kong-based commercial landlord and developer said in a statement on Friday.
The transfer of these assets to the fund came following the lapse of pre-emptive offers by Hongkong Land to its joint venture partners, it said.
“The fund will be solely focused on managing prime commercial property assets in Singapore, creating a unique private investment platform that will own and operate some of Singapore’s most valuable real estate assets in terms of location, tenants and resilience of rental income,” Hongkong Land said, adding that over time the fund would create earnings growth for the company.
One Raffles Link, a seven-storey office building solely owned by Hongkong Land, would also form part of the fund’s assets, it added.
One Raffles Link, Marina Bay Financial Centre Towers 1 and 2 and One Raffles Quay had a total attributable property value of S$3.9 billion as of June and represented 3.2 million sq ft of prime office space in Singapore, the company said.
Meanwhile, Hongkong Land sold its one-third stake in Marina Bay Financial Centre Tower 3 to Keppel Reit for about S$1.5 billion, which was 2 per cent above the valuation of the property as of June, the developer said.
Net proceeds from the sale of Tower 3 would increase Hongkong Land’s total capital recycling achieved since 2024 to US$2.8 billion from US$2.1 billion – around 70 per cent of its 2027 target of US$4 billion, it said.

SCPREF is expected to launch with more than double the assets of Hongkong Land’s seed portfolio, the developer said. “The level of equity commitments by third-party capital investors into SCPREF is currently in the final stage of documentation,” it added.
The establishment of the fund aligned with the company’s strategy to grow its assets under management to US$100 billion by 2035, with meaningful participation from third-party capital investors, it said.
“Singapore remains a core market for Hongkong Land, with the company planning to use the capital recycled from the sale...to further invest in ultra-premium integrated commercial properties,” the statement said.
Keppel Reit CEO Chua Hsien Yang said the real estate investment trust’s purchase of Hongkong Land’s stake in the 46-storey Tower 3 presented “a rare opportunity to increase our interest in an iconic asset in the prime Marina Bay area”.
“This acquisition reflects our confidence in Singapore’s prime office sector and reinforces our focus to anchor and continue to grow our portfolio of grade-A commercial assets in the market.”
In connection with the acquisition, Keppel Reit launched an underwritten non-renounceable preferential offering of new units to raise gross proceeds of about S$886 million, which it said would be used to partially fund the purchase and its related costs.

data.title: UK ‘super flu’ outbreak sparks ‘worst-case’ fears amid looming Christmas doctors’ strike
data.description:
The UK is facing an “unprecedented wave of super flu”, a health chief warned on Friday as the health minister urged doctors to call off a threatened five-day strike ahead of Christmas.
Wes Streeting said the state-funded National Health Service (NHS) was in an “incredibly precarious situation” as flu cases mounted, and was facing a “challenge unlike any it has seen since the pandemic”.
NHS figures published on Thursday showed flu cases at a record level for the time of year.
The number of cases jumped 55 per cent in a week to an average of 2,660 patients in hospital each day last week.
“With record demand … and an impending resident [junior)]doctors strike, this unprecedented wave of super flu is leaving the NHS facing a worst-case scenario for this time of year,” said NHS National Medical Director Meghana Pandit.
Streeting said the numbers could triple before the peak and said the situation in hospitals was already “inexcusable”.
“That’s why I am appealing directly to resident doctors to accept the government’s offer,” he wrote in The Times newspaper.
The functioning of the NHS is a major political issue in Britain, with Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s beleaguered Labour government under pressure to bring down waiting times.
The planned strike from Wednesday will be the 14th walkout by doctors since March 2023, if it goes ahead.

Attempts to reduce patient waiting lists have been affected in part by the repeated industrial action by both resident doctors and consultants.
The resident doctors – those below consultant level – are in dispute with the government over pay and a shortage of training opportunities.
Streeting has agreed to the doctors’ union’s demand that UK-trained doctors get priority for training posts over candidates from overseas.
The number of training places will also be boosted.
But he insists the government “cannot and will not move on pay, especially not after a 28.9 per cent pay rise over the last three years and the highest pay award across the entire public sector in the last two”.
The British Medical Association, which represents resident doctors, is demanding an extra 26 per cent it says is needed to achieve pay restoration after years of below-inflation pay deals.
It is due to put the government’s new deal to members in an online survey, which will close on Monday.

data.title: Why Steven Spielberg’s black drama The Color Purple remains controversial 40 years on
data.description:
This is the latest instalment in our From the Vault feature series, in which we reflect on culturally significant movies celebrating notable anniversaries.
When Steven Spielberg was announced as the director of The Color Purple (1985), which turns 40 this month, few thought he was the right person for the job. Not even, it seems, Spielberg himself.
Alice Walker’s Pulitzer Prize-winning 1982 novel concerns the plight of poor African-Americans in early 20th-century Georgia.
“You need a black director for this,” Spielberg told producer Quincy Jones. The response? “Did you have to be an alien to direct E.T.?”
Whatever his suitability for the role, Spielberg’s intentions were honourable. He waived his usual US$15 million fee for the US$40,000 Directors Guild of America (DGA) minimum and cast unknowns in the leads.


Walker wrote the first draft of the screenplay, which was later revised by Dutch writer Menno Meyjes, but Walker remained on board throughout, helping the actors nail the tricky Southern dialect.
The story follows Celie (played by Desreta Jackson as a child, then Whoopi Goldberg), a young woman whose life is shaped by systemic cruelty. Abused by her father, she has her children taken from her, then is forced to marry the violent Mister (Danny Glover).
Mister mistreats her, drives her sister Nettie (Akosua Busia) away – hiding the letters she sends – and openly favours his mistress, the glamorous blues singer Shug (Margaret Avery).
Over the years, as Celie waits for word from Nettie, she slowly learns to stand up for herself. “I don’t know how to fight,” she says at one point. “All I know how to do is stay alive.”

The performers are uniformly excellent, particularly breakthrough stars Goldberg and Oprah Winfrey, who plays the imperious Sofia, a woman who refuses to bow to anyone – defiance that proves to be her undoing in the film’s most affecting sequence.
Spielberg’s famous craft is also much in evidence. No matter how pathetic Mister is, he always looms large and imposing in the frame. And the postbox becomes a symbol of stillborn hope, as the camera zooms into its mouth looking for a letter that will never come.
Yet, despite the craft and deep feeling, there are frequently times when something seems slightly off. Jones’ score is too syrupy, and Allen Daviau’s cinematography is almost too pretty: for example, the scene where Nettie is dragged, kicking and screaming, from Mister’s property was shot at magic hour – the last part of the day before sunset – bathing the violence in golden light.

You would need a heart of stone not to be moved by Celie’s plight, but tonally the film is all over the place, with scenes of broad comedy clattering into sudden tragedy.
And Spielberg’s direction sometimes slips into pastiche; the juke-joint sequences feel borrowed from old movies, rather than any kind of lived experience.
Upon release, the film faced criticism for the way it depicted most of its black male characters as violent abusers. But this is a problem inherent in slimming novels down to a manageable length for a movie – a lot of nuance is lost in the edit.
“It’s one woman’s story,” said Winfrey at the time. “It was not meant to be the history of every black man or woman in this country and I wish they’d just shut up about it.”

Spielberg said: “Most of the criticism came from directors [who] felt that we had overlooked them, and that it should have been a black director telling a black story. That was the main criticism.
“The other criticism was that I had softened the book. I have always copped to that. I made the movie I wanted to make from Alice Walker’s book.”
He would receive support from an unlikely source: Platoon director Oliver Stone, hardly known for his bleeding heart.
“It’s an excellent movie,” said Stone. “And it wouldn’t have been done if it hadn’t been Spielberg. It’s not like everyone says, that he ruined the book. That’s horses**t. Nobody was going to do the book. He made the book live again.”

Stone is probably right. Hollywood in the 1980s was not exactly overrun with filmmakers rushing to tell authentic African-American stories, and it would be another two decades before anyone returned to Walker’s source material. Even then, Blitz Bazawule’s well-regarded 2023 musical made relatively few ripples.
Looking back, The Color Purple is perhaps more interesting as a pivot point in Spielberg’s career than as a film in its own right. This new, serious-minded director would go on to make the likes of Schindler’s List and Saving Private Ryan, leaving children’s entertainment behind.
The establishment agreed. The Color Purple received 11 Academy Award nominations, including best picture. It won none, holding the dubious joint record – with the 1977 film The Turning Point – for the most nominations without a single victory.
It also holds another distinction: no film has ever received so many nods without its director even securing a nomination.
Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook

data.title: Alipay and Rokid open up ‘look-and-pay’ smart glasses function to developers
data.description:
Alipay and artificial intelligence glasses maker Rokid have jointly unveiled a smart glasses payment solution on the Rokid Lingzhu AI development platform, giving developers an easy path to build payment functions into their applications.
Based on the Model Context Protocol (MCP), an open-source standard that connects AI applications to external systems, the service allowed developers to easily integrate pre-built capabilities, such as receiving payments, by adding plug-ins, according to a statement from Ant Group on Wednesday.
The function has been made available to participants in the Rokid Spatial Joy 25 Global AR and AI Developer Competition.
End users can pay directly using natural language phrases, a core functionality demonstrated by the “look-and-pay” feature of Alipay on Rokid smart glasses. The feature enables hands-free transactions via voice command and eye-gaze directed at a merchant’s Alipay QR code or “tap-to-pay” device.

Alipay is operated by Ant Group, an affiliate of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the Post.
Alibaba showcased the Quark AI glasses and look-and-pay function in July in Shanghai at the World Artificial Intelligence Conference. Alibaba launched its Quark AI glasses last month, joining a slew of Chinese technology firms in the smart glasses sector.
The look-and-pay function was later expanded to Xiaomi and RayNeo AI glasses, as the payment option continues to gain traction along with methods using QR codes and facial recognition.
Ant International, Ant Group’s overseas arm, recently unveiled its iris-authentication payment technology for smart glasses.
To capture the emerging commercial opportunities presented by AI agents, Chinese technology companies are rapidly adopting the MCP framework. This framework standardises the integration of AI agents – autonomous programmes that perform specific tasks – with external data and tools.
Tbox, Ant Group’s AI agent development platform, supports the deployment of more than 30 MCP services, such as Alipay, Amap and Wuying, according to Alibaba. Alibaba Cloud, the company’s cloud division, launched an MCP marketplace via its AI model hosting platform ModelScope.
Yuanqi, the AI agent development platform of Tencent Cloud, the cloud computing unit of Chinese social media and gaming giant Tencent Holdings, integrated WeChat Pay MCP in July.

data.title: Taipei mayor Chiang Wan-an to attend annual forum in Shanghai under Taiwan’s watch
data.description:
The mayor of Taipei is set to travel to Shanghai for a rare joint forum while being watched closely by Taiwan’s ruling Democratic Progressive Party (DPP).
Chiang Wan-an, mayor of Taipei, will travel to Shanghai on December 27 and 28 for the Shanghai-Taipei City Forum, Taiwan’s Central News Agency said on Thursday.
On Friday, Xinhua stated that the annual forum, carrying the theme “Technology Transforming Life”, would convene in Shanghai on December 28.
The forum, which was first held in 2010, will run for one day, matching the duration of last year’s event in Taipei.


Taiwan’s China Times reported that Chiang told the Taipei City Council on Thursday his Shanghai itinerary would “not involve politics”.
On Thursday afternoon, Liang Wen-chieh, the spokesman for Taiwan’s Mainland Affairs Council (MAC), told the Taipei city government its delegation should “focus on municipal exchanges” in Shanghai.
He said the council must be notified in advance of any deviation from the pre-approved itinerary and that Taipei’s delegation must gain approval from the island’s highest administrative authority before signing any cooperation agreement with Shanghai.
Liang added that the Taipei delegation’s two-day itinerary was determined “based on considerations and decisions by the Taipei city government” and was “unrelated to the political atmosphere”.
The forum arrives at a tense moment in cross-strait relations, most recently marked by Taiwan authorities’ ban on the mainland Chinese social media app RedNote, which drew sharp criticism from Beijing and backlash within Taiwan.
It is also a rare venue attended by officials from both sides of the strait.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
Since the DPP came to power in 2016, Beijing has increased military and diplomatic pressure on Taiwan to deter what it says are pro-independence forces in the island, particularly after William Lai Ching-te became Taiwan’s leader last year.
Beijing has repeatedly accused Lai of being a “separatist” and “troublemaker”.
Taiwan’s United Daily News quoted Chiang on Wednesday as saying that if the forum were cancelled, “the message conveyed would make everyone more pessimistic about cross-strait relations”.
United Daily News said this year’s forum had originally been scheduled for September but was postponed until late this month because Taiwan had not completed its review of memorandums of understanding that are expected to be signed at the event.
Taiwanese media said the two cities would sign memorandums of understanding on water management and vocational training during the forum.
Representatives from both cities would exchange views on topics including “technology and healthcare, rail transit, and health and elderly care”, Xinhua said.
Beijing’s Taiwan Affairs Office spokesman Chen Binhua said on Wednesday that Taipei and Shanghai had “maintained communication” regarding the forum’s organisation, adding that Beijing “consistently supports exchanges and cooperation across all fields, including cross-strait city-to-city interactions”.
In December last year, Shanghai vice-mayor Hua Yuan led a delegation to the forum in Taipei, staying for less than two days in what was the first visit to the island by a senior official from the city in six years.


The Shanghai-Taipei City Forum, which began in 2010, is attended by senior officials from both sides, with the aim of deepening exchanges and cooperation.
However, the two official delegations have not always been of an equal administrative level, prompting criticism from DPP lawmakers.
Shanghai’s mayor has attended all the forums held in the mainland financial centre.
But Han Zheng is the only mayor to have also attended the Taipei event, which he did for its launch in 2010.
Han is now China’s Vice-President.
Other forums held in Taiwan were attended by a delegation led by one of Shanghai’s vice-mayors.
All forums held in both Shanghai and Taipei have been attended by the mayor of Taipei, including former mayors Ko Wen-je and Hau Lung-pin.

data.title: Japan finally airs Oscar-nominated #MeToo film ‘Black Box Diaries’ after lengthy delay
data.description:
A Japanese #MeToo campaigner’s documentary premiered in her homeland on Friday after months of delays, with one audience member saying she hoped it triggered a change in society.
Shiori Ito won a landmark 2019 civil case against a Japanese TV reporter accused of raping her – a charge he denies – and turned her ordeal into a film released last year and screened worldwide.
But some segments of Black Box Diaries contained video and audio that had been clandestinely shot or meant only for use in court, resulting in its Japan release being significantly delayed.
Publicist Toei Advertising announced last month the Oscar-nominated film would finally be shown in Japan after “revisions and adjustments” had been made.
“I’ve spent the last 10 years making this film thinking it was going to be a love letter to Japan,” Ito told a screening event at a Tokyo cinema on Friday.

“It means a lot to me that this film was released in Japan, where I grew up and where I want people to face this issue,” she said.
Ito, who received vicious online criticism for going public, had previously said that despite several high-profile cases, Japan has never seen an outpouring of #MeToo allegations.
Government surveys in Japan show few rape victims report the crime to the police, although the number of consultations at sexual violence support centres is rising.
Black Box Diaries tugged at the heartstrings of many who watched it on Friday at a cinema in Tokyo.
“I couldn’t watch it without crying. It’s a wonder that she is alive today, and I have nothing but full respect,” said Yuko Ono, a publishing house employee in her 60s.


‘Suffered in silence’
The film alleged that police attempts to arrest ex-journalist Noriyuki Yamaguchi, who Ito accuses of raping her in 2015, were aborted at the last minute at the order of “higher-ups”.
Yamaguchi is known for having had close links with then-prime minister Shinzo Abe.
“She persistently forced open the monolithic police organisation – that can greatly empower all these people who have suffered in silence,” Ono said.
Niko Nagata, a 20-year-old university student, agreed.
“Sexual violence is a particular kind of crime with high risks of re-victimisation, and so-called rape myths remain strong,” she said.

“I hope the film will hammer home the severity of sexual violence to many, and trigger a change in society.”
Ito also acknowledged that “various opinions” surrounding the documentary “made me feel very apologetic”.
Some revisions were made to the latest edition screened, including editing out scenes and rendering some individuals unidentifiable, the director explained in a statement on Friday.
Ito’s former lawyer, Yoko Nishihiro, said in a statement reported by local media on Thursday that the film was “freighted with serious human rights issues”.
The lawyer and her team previously highlighted a secretly recorded phone conversation and other footage used without permission, including hotel CCTV shown in court.

data.title: Indonesia urged to tread carefully as Russia courts Prabowo with energy and defence offers
data.description:
Indonesia should tread carefully before deepening ties with Russia, analysts said, after President Prabowo Subianto made an unplanned visit to Moscow this week, raising questions about the diplomatic and economic costs of closer engagement – including how Washington could respond.
Russian President Vladimir Putin welcomed Prabowo on Wednesday, hailing 75 years of what the Kremlin described as “solid and steady” diplomatic relations between the two countries.
The visit was Prabowo’s second to Russia this year, following his participation in an economic forum in St Petersburg in June, and came as Moscow stepped up overtures on energy, trade and military cooperation.
During the meeting, Putin offered Russian assistance to help Indonesia develop its energy sector, particularly nuclear power plants, alongside increased wheat shipments.

“Our two countries have significant potential in energy, including nuclear power. I know that your country has plans in this regard. As for us, we remain at your disposal should you wish to reach out to our specialists,” Putin said on Wednesday.
Putin noted that Indonesia “enjoys a surplus” in its trade balance with Russia, particularly in the agricultural sector, but “we are not complaining”.
“There was a slight decrease in wheat exports to your market, I believe, which will be another topic during our conversation today,” Putin said.
The Russian leader also confirmed that talks are “under way” between the Eurasian Economic Union and Indonesia to create a free-trade zone.
On defence cooperation, Putin said Russia was “ready to expand” military ties with Indonesia, including in training Indonesian specialists in Russian universities and military academies.
Prabowo responded that he “appreciated” the offers, which underlined the “very strong” bilateral relations between their countries.
He said he had recently hosted several Russian delegations in Jakarta and linked them with “various Indonesian associations and institutions that can act as partners for further coordination”.
Prabowo also invited Putin to visit Indonesia, possibly in 2026 or 2027, an offer the Russian leader accepted, saying, “I will certainly do that.”
Energy appeal
Putin’s proposal on nuclear energy aligns with Prabowo’s ambition to add more than 100 gigawatts of power capacity over the next 15 years, including 5 gigawatts from nuclear energy.
Radityo Dharmaputra, a lecturer in international relations at Airlangga University, said that “it is indeed very tempting” to team up with Russia to develop nuclear power plants.
“The problem is, with economic sanctions against Russia, why risk making it difficult to transact?” Radityo told This Week in Asia.
“What Russia is offering is a package deal of liquefied natural gas and nuclear [plants], and that does seem cheaper. However, we can get LNG from the US, and the government needs to consider how [the sanctions on Russia will affect] the future development of a nuclear plant.”
Those sanctions, imposed by the US, EU and their allies after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022, restrict Russian banks, energy firms and technology transfers, potentially complicating financing, payments and long-term support for major projects even for countries not formally party to the measures.


Muhammad Habib Abiyan Dzakwan, a researcher at the Centre for Strategic and International Studies Indonesia, questioned whether there had been “a comprehensive study” of the risks and costs of a closer partnership with Moscow.
“There are certain trade partners whose commodities are good for domestic industry, but cannot be used as export components for third-party markets,” Muhammad said. “All countries can provide us with opportunities, but have we studied the risks and costs that will arise from any negotiations or commitments to trade agreements with these countries?”
It was impossible to say now whether closer ties with the Kremlin would irk Washington, given shifting geopolitical dynamics, he added.
“I believe that Indonesian technocrats and diplomats are still very capable of [assessing] these matters,” Muhammad said.
Radityo argued that Prabowo may have been using the Moscow visit to nudge Washington towards greater flexibility in trade negotiations, but added that “Russia has nothing to offer in the context of trade, so it’s difficult to use it as a bargaining chip”.
While Russia is the world’s largest wheat exporter, Muhammad noted that Australia remains Indonesia’s biggest supplier, particularly since Moscow launched its invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
In 2024, Russia exported 1.3 million tonnes of wheat to Indonesia worth more than US$310 million, according to Agroexport, Russia’s agricultural export agency.
That figure pales beside Australia’s wheat exports to Indonesia, valued at AU$1.76 billion (US$1.17 billion) between 2023 and 2024, according to Australia’s agriculture ministry.
Backtracking on US talks
Prabowo’s visit to Russia came amid reports that trade talks between Jakarta and Washington were on the brink of collapsing as US officials told Reuters on Wednesday that their Indonesian counterparts had “backtracked” on several commitments.

Unnamed US officials also told the Financial Times that Indonesia had reneged on agreements to eliminate non-tariff barriers on American industrial and agricultural products, as well as on digital trade issues.
In July, the White House said Indonesia agreed to scrap import tariffs on 99 per cent of American goods and to buy US$15 billion in US energy and US$4.5 billion in American agricultural products.
In exchange, Washington reduced its reciprocal tariffs on Indonesian goods to 19 per cent from an initial 32 per cent.
Haryo Limanseto, a spokesman with the Coordinating Ministry of Economic Affairs, said in a statement on Wednesday that trade negotiations with the US “are still ongoing” and that “there are no specific issues in the negotiations”.
Anne Patricia Sutanto, head of the trade affairs division at the Indonesian Employers Association, said Washington may have sought to include a controversial provision similar to those in recent US agreements with Malaysia and Cambodia that critics say could compel partner countries to align with US economic restrictions or sanctions on third parties.
“Whatever trade deals we commit to with other countries, we must not violate our commitments. We have bilateral and multilateral agreements with other countries, so we cannot [abandon] them just because of one country,” Sutanto said.
She urged Jakarta to keep negotiating with Washington while also diversifying export markets “so that we do not depend on the US”.

data.title: Provocative Japanese actions risk plunging Asia into peril: China’s PLA Daily
data.description:
China’s military mouthpiece warned on Friday that provocative actions by right-wing Japanese politicians risked plunging Asia into peril, as Beijing prepared to mark the 88th anniversary of the Nanking massacre amid heightened tensions with Tokyo.
PLA Daily published the commentary as sirens are poised to wail across Nanjing, the capital of eastern Jiangsu province, at 10.01am on Saturday for the national memorial ceremony led by the Central Committee and the State Council.
Introduced by China’s top legislature in 2014, the commemoration day has been a source of bitter grievance in China about wartime history.
According to China’s official estimate, more than 300,000 civilians and soldiers were killed in the six weeks after Japanese troops entered Nanking, now called Nanjing, on December 13, 1937. Japan disputes the number of fatalities.
In the commentary titled “Never Allow the Tragedies of History to Repeat Themselves”, author Xu Yizhen decried Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s November 7 remarks to Japan’s parliament suggesting a Taiwan contingency could be a “survival-threatening situation” for Japan as a blatant historical inversion that revived the spectre of militarism.

Xu said the Japanese leader had “openly turned back the wheel of history: first by absurdly claiming that a Taiwan contingency could constitute an existential crisis for Japan and then by trying to stir up” the notion of the island’s undetermined status.
The notion, which asserts that the ultimate sovereignty of Taiwan is legally unresolved, is strongly opposed by Beijing.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US and its treaty ally Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state. But Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
In the commentary, Xu stated that “any appeasement or tolerance of these provocative moves by Japanese right-wing politicians will only allow the ghost of militarism to rise again and once more place the peoples of Asia in peril”.
“All countries have both the responsibility and the obligation to join hands to stop any attempt to revive militarism and fascism and to jointly safeguard the hard-won fruits of victory” of the second world war, he added.
Takaichi’s remarks have ignited a full-blown diplomatic crisis, plunging Sino-Japanese ties to a new low and triggering a series of retaliatory measures from Beijing, which considers the remarks an interference in its internal affairs and a departure from Tokyo’s long-term policy of strategic ambiguity on Taiwan.
Bilateral frictions flared again following a mid-air stand-off between Chinese and Japanese fighter jets over the weekend.
Japan blamed Chinese J-15 aircraft from the Liaoning aircraft carrier for locking fire-control radar onto Japanese F-15 jets while the carrier fleet was conducting exercises in international airspace near Okinawa. Beijing accused Tokyo of deliberately disrupting its normal and lawful military exercises and escalating tensions.
Meanwhile, on Friday, Chinese foreign ministry spokesman Guo Jiakun said the Nanking massacre remained “one of the darkest pages in human history”.
“We will never allow the right-wing forces in Japan to reverse the course of history, never allow external forces to interfere with China’s Taiwan region, and never allow the resurgence of Japanese militarism,” Guo said at a press conference.
This year’s observances coincide with China marking the 80th anniversary of the end of World War II and the global victory against fascism.
Multiple events are scheduled to be held in Jiangsu on Saturday, including visits to surviving victims, an event at a former refuge shelter, the release of newly collected historical artefacts and materials, and a documentary exhibition of the atrocities, according to the provincial government.
In Hong Kong, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu is expected to attend a memorial ceremony on Saturday and lay a wreath in honour of the victims.
Simultaneous memorial activities will take place at anti-Japanese war-related patriotic education bases across China on Saturday. In total, 100 primary schools around the country will sit the 2025 “Learn History, Recite Peace – Joint Reading” online class to remember the history, China News Service reported on Friday.

data.title: What to do in Hong Kong this Christmas: Michelin-star feasts, circus acts and charity events
data.description:
As the year draws to a close, Hong Kong is awash with set menus, cultural happenings, themed brunches and extravagant countdown parties. The offerings run the gamut from high tradition (yes, more turkey) to the decidedly unconventional (fried chicken, anyone?).
Tai Kwun has launched its “Simple Gifts of Joy” celebration, running until January 4. The main event is “Circus Plays”, a programme bringing in high-calibre international circus acts, including the Asia premiere of The Genesis from Denmark and the Hong Kong premiere of Spiral Slide from the United States.

Focusing on the philanthropic, Upper House has an art-led tree in collaboration with artist Cj Hendry and auction house Phillips. It’s composed of 200 oversized plush green “juju” toys, each “adoptable” for HK$688, with all proceeds benefiting the charity Mother’s Choice.
If you’re suffering from turkey fatigue, May Chow’s Little Bao is throwing the rule book out the kitchen window with its Sichuan-style fried chicken served with a crème fraîche and ikura dip. It headlines an eight-course festive set that also includes a 24-hour sous-vide short rib bao and a rich bowl of chicken innard noodles.

For those who demand classic luxury, the city’s fine-dining stalwarts are obliging. At The Landmark Mandarin Oriental, the three-Michelin-starred Amber has eight-course festive dinners on the cards, while the Michelin-starred Beefbar is offering six-course menus for Christmas and New Year’s Eve.
Those with more contemporary tastes might venture out to Mosu Hong Kong for a festive lunch or dinner featuring a minimalist Korean degustation, or to Kanesaka, which has a Christmas omakase dinner bringing in the best catch from Japanese waters.

For something a notch below the multi-Michelin-star stratosphere, the all-day brasseries are your best bet. Upper House’s Salisterra is covering all bases with festive set lunches, dinners and a special Christmas Day menu. Just a hop and a skip away, The Continental is offering four-course dinners on Christmas Eve, Boxing Day and New Year’s Eve.
Whatever your inclination this December, it’s clear there is no shortage of ways to celebrate the festive season this year.
Festive spreads, delivered
Prefer to celebrate at home? The city’s holiday takeaway menus have you sorted.
Petrus

At Island Shangri-La’s Petrus, chef Uwe Opocensky’s HK$8,888 holiday meal is built around his golden roast butterball turkey with traditional trimmings. For smaller celebrations, the premium roast turkey gift box (HK$2,288) offers all the trimmings of a five-star feast. shangri-la.com
Heimat

Heimat leans into continental comfort this year, with chef Peter Find’s German-style takeaway featuring roast goose (HK$1,280) and duck (HK$980) with potato dumplings, plus add-ons (from HK$80) such as sausage platters and vegetable sides. heimat.hk
Market Place

Market Place’s 12-person Christmas Set (HK$2,528) goes generous with roasted chicken, slow-cooked oxtail, lobster and roasted vegetables. The six-person set (HK$1,199) keeps things cosy with lamb rack, pork knuckle and seasonal sides. marketplacehk.com
Butterfly Patisserie

For sweets, Rosewood Hong Kong’s Butterfly Patisserie offers a series of seasonal creations by executive pastry chef Jonathan Soukdeo, including a Mont Blanc Christmas log cake (HK$588) and ornament-inspired pastries and matcha chiffon rolls (HK$98). rosewoodhotels.com
